narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 grade decile of 6, Year 3 decile of 1, and plan to attend part-time—and calculated a 43% probability that you would fail the bar exam. Since this risk threshold triggered an automatic rejection, your application was denied to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 1, which is substantially lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled a significant drop-off in performance compared to peers. This stood out as the top driver because it strongly indicated challenges in sustaining high achievement, a key predictor of bar passage. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 6—which is slightly above the average of 5.71—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing by showing early promise that many students don't match. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, below the typical 3.21, also pulled positively but with less force, suggesting it helped somewhat but couldn't offset the bigger concerns. Overall, while your LSAT and part-time status played roles, it was this combination—dominated by the sharp decline to a low Year 3 ranking—that set you apart from typical admitted students who maintain consistent or improving deciles. Your profile compares to the average by having a solid start but faltering later, unlike most who climb steadily toward bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted coursework or tutoring, and exploring full-time options since only 7.3% of students pursue part-time paths successfully. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to admission. Many students in similar situations have rebounded by focusing on these core areas—your upper-middle background gives you resources to invest in that preparation. Keep pushing; this is just one data point, not your full story.
